elements.sent-2411-001: Generating large-scale, domain-specific, multilingual multi-turn dialogue datasets remains a significant hurdle for training effective Multi-Turn Intent Classification models in chatbot systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we introduce Chain-of-Intent, a novel mechanism that combines Hidden Markov Models with Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate contextually aware, intent-driven conversations through self-play.
elements.sent-2411-003: By extracting domain-specific knowledge from e-commerce chat logs, we estimate conversation turns and intent transitions, which guide the generation of coherent dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-004: Leveraging LLMs to enhance emission probabilities, our approach produces natural and contextually consistent questions and answers.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also propose MINT-CL, a framework for multi-turn intent classification using multi-task contrastive learning, improving classification accuracy without the need for extensive annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Evaluations show that our methods outperform baselines in dialogue quality and intent classification accuracy, especially in multilingual settings, while significantly reducing data generation efforts.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, we release MINT-E, a multilingual, intent-aware multi-turn e-commerce dialogue corpus to support future research in this area.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.45\textwidth]{figures/challenges.png} \caption{Example of intent-aware multi-turn dialogue and the challenges of tagging single-turn cross-market intents to create multi-turn intent database. The intents (e.g. Chitchat - Complaint) are in the format of <intent type>-<intent name>.} \label{fig:challenge}
elements.sent-2411-008: Chatbots are essential on international e-commerce platforms, offering 24/7 customer service.
elements.sent-2411-009: Users typically engage with these chatbots through multi-turn conversations to achieve their goals.
elements.sent-2411-010: Therefore, it is crucial for the chatbot to accurately identify the user's intent at each turn using a Multi-Turn Intent Classification (MTIC) model.
elements.sent-2411-011: To improve accuracy, MTIC models should consider not just the current message but also previous interactions and intents.
elements.sent-2411-012: Ignoring the context from earlier turns often leads to mistakes, giving irrelevant answers and making users dissatisfied~\cite{xu2014contextual .. Motivation and Challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Similar to many deep learning approaches~\cite{Soudani2024ASO , MTIC models heavily rely on large amounts of domain-specific multi-turn intent-utterance pairs to boost their performance~\cite{ni2023recent .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, gathering such datasets efficiently, especially across different languages, is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-015: While some studies on dialogue understanding in multi-turn intent classification~\cite{wu2021context,qu2019user,ren2020intention assume there is plenty of multi-turn training data available, that is often not the case in reality.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recent work~\cite{zhao2023chatgpt shows that using few-shot prompting with ChatGPT does not perform well for intent classification, and it does not match up to supervised methods.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Figure~\ref{fig:challenge illustrates the difficulties in annotating multi-turn dialogues.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For MTIC in information-seeking dialogues~\cite{Askari2024SelfseedingAM , manual annotation of intents is time-consuming and resource-intensive, which leads to a lack of training data.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Unlike popular intent classification datasets such as MSdialog-Intent~\cite{Qu2018AnalyzingAC , which has only 12 unique intents, industrial chatbots handle hundreds of intents to meet specific user needs in each market.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This vast number of intents makes classification tasks and data annotation more complex.
elements.sent-2411-021: With so many options, annotators can easily make errors and need more time to make the right decisions, making the process expensive and slow.
elements.sent-2411-022: For instance, \citet{qu2019user reported that labelling intents for 2,199 dialogues cost \$1,700.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Manually annotating large-scale, multi-turn datasets in multiple languages or constantly updating the model for new user intents is impractical.
elements.sent-2411-024: Lack of sufficient training data often leads to poorer performance, highlighting the need for a better way to address this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-025: Most research integrates the dialogue acts (DA) for domain-specific task-oriented dialogue (TOD) generation \cite{Wang2020MultiDomainDA .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, dialogue acts are more general, like statement/request/yes-answer, and therefore unusable for domain-specific generation.
elements.sent-2411-027: Recent works also adopt specific DA in the e-commerce domain for only TOD intents \cite{Chen2019TheJC .}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, there are FAQ, chit-chat, and TOD intents in multi-turn sessions shown in Figure ~\ref{fig:challenge , only considering that TOD intents are limited for dialogue generation in real-world scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-029: To tackle these issues, this paper contributes three solutions from different aspects, as follows: Contribution 1: Chain-of-Intent.
elements.sent-2411-030: We introduce Chain-of-Intent, a novel method for generating intent-aware dialogues through self-play.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our approach combines Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) to model intent sequences with Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce coherent, context-aware multi-turn dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-032: By extracting domain-specific knowledge from historical chat logs, we generate realistic intent sequences and leverage LLMs to create high-quality multilingual dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-033: This method addresses the data scarcity problem by generating large-scale, domain-specific dialogue datasets without extensive manual annotation.. Contribution 2: MINT-CL.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose MINT-CL, a framework for MTIC with multi-task contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-035: MINT-CL enhances the accuracy of MTIC models by leveraging both current and contextual information and optimizing the model with a multi-task contrastive learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our experiments demonstrate that MINT-CL outperforms baseline models across various languages and markets.. Contribution 3: MINT-E.
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, we release MINT-E, a multilingual intent-aware multi-turn e-commerce dialogues corpus, to support research in this field.
elements.sent-2411-038: The corpus covers eight different markets with diverse languages and a large number of intents (as shown in Table~\ref{table:data_train_test ), providing a valuable resource for developing and evaluating MTIC models.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In a closed domain question answering (QA) system, we have a set of predetermined intent classes $\mathcal{I = \{I_i\}_{i=1 ^k$, ranging from informational queries to action-oriented requests.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Here, we assume that a dataset of single-turn question-intent pairs $\mathcal{D = {(x_i, y_i) $ can be easily accessible, where $x_i$ denotes the question and $y_i \in \mathcal{I}$ is its labelled intent.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This dataset is described as single-turn because all $x_i$ on their own are standalone complete utterances and are semantically clear for intent inference.
elements.sent-2411-042: The key difference between single-turn and multi-turn QA is the presence of history.
elements.sent-2411-043: In a multi-turn conversation $\mathcal{C}$ with number of turns $t$, we have an alternating sequence of $t$ question and answer pairs, and each question is associated with an intent.
elements.sent-2411-044: Formally, $\mathcal{C = \{(q_1, I_1, a_1),..., (q_{t , I_{t , a_{t )\}$, where $q_t$ is the question at $t$-th turn, $I_t$ is its intent, and $a_t$ is the corresponding answer.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: We now define the task for e-commerce multi-turn conversation question generation.
elements.sent-2411-046: Given an intent $I_t$, and a conversation history $h_t = \{(q_1, a_1),..., (q_{t-1 , a_{t-1 )\}$, the multi-turn conversation question generation task aims to generate a question $q_t$ at $t$-th turn: \begin{equation}q_t = \arg \max_q P(q | I_t, h_t)\end{equation} in which the generated question should be coherent with the conversation history and natural like in human conversation.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.48\textwidth]{figures/Methods.png} \caption{Detailed illustration of using LLM-enhanced HMM to sample a chain of intents and generate intent-aware multi-turn dialogues} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-047: Figure~\ref{fig:method shows the detail workflow of intent-aware dialogue generation, which comprises two main stages: domain knowledge extraction and question generation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: As LLMs do not possess domain-specific knowledge out-of-the-box, this step is imperative to extract the knowledge to be injected into the conversation generation process, bridging the gap for business usage.
elements.sent-2411-049: There are two domain-specific knowledge to account for when generating a conversation, namely the number of turns in each conversation and the intent transition from intents of historical questions.
elements.sent-2411-050: These two combined will guide the generation of each conversation.
elements.sent-2411-051: From the real-life chat logs between the platform user and agent, the probability distribution of the number of turns of each conversation $P(T)$ is observed.
elements.sent-2411-052: Before generating a conversation, the number of turns in the conversation $T$ is first decided by sampling from $P(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Other than the number of turns, we also need to know the sensible sequence of intents that users might have in a conversation.
elements.sent-2411-054: Just like the number of turns, the intent transition information is also gathered from the chat logs.
elements.sent-2411-055: One limitation here is that the intent from chat logs is inferred by an intent recognition model trained on the single-turn data $\mathcal{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Each $q_t$ is inferred separately without considering $h_t$ to obtain $I_t$ for the $t$-th turn.
elements.sent-2411-057: That said, by the Law of Large Numbers, if we gather the statistics from a reasonable large number of chat logs, the intent transition matrix obtained will provide a good estimation to true intents.
elements.sent-2411-058: Hereby, the initial intent distribution $P(I_1)$ is specified in a row matrix, while the intent transition $P(I_t|I_{t-1 )$ is defined in another $|\mathcal{I | \times |\mathcal{I |$ matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Note that the $P(I_t|I_{t-1 )$ here is time invariant, the probability of $P(I_j|I_i)$ will be the same regardless of $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Using this statistical approach, we are able to sample a probabilistic chain of intent with the sampled number of turns and intent transition matrix.
elements.sent-2411-061: The sampled chain of intent plays a crucial role in the following question generation process.
elements.sent-2411-062: We introduce Chain-of-Intent, an effective method for self-play generation of intent-aware dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our approach utilizes a HMM to generate sequences of user intents in specific domains and integrates LLMs to produce coherent and context-aware dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-064: The HMM models the user's interaction with the chatbot, where the intents are the hidden states and the utterances are the observations.
elements.sent-2411-065: To capture realistic intent flows within specific domains, we extract domain knowledge from historical chat logs by calculating:.
elements.sent-2411-066: The turn distribution $P(T)$: the probability distribution of the number of turns in a conversation.
elements.sent-2411-067: The initial intent distribution $P_{\text{init }$: the probability distribution of intents at the first turn.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The intent transition distribution $P_{\text{trans }$: the probability of transitioning from one intent to another.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Using these distributions, we sample the number of turns $T$ from $P(T)$, the initial intent $I_1$ from $P_{\text{init }$, and subsequent intents $I_t$ from $P_{\text{trans }$ based on $I_{t-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Formally, we define the HMM as: \begin{equation}P(S_{1:T}, Y_{1:T}) = P(S_1) P(Y_1|S_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(S_t|S_{t-1}) P(Y_t|S_t)\end{equation} In our case, $S_t$ corresponds to the intent $I_t$, and $Y_t$ corresponds to the user's utterance (question) at turn $t$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The observation model $P(Y_t|S_t)$ is modeled by uniformly sampling a question $X_t$ from the set of questions in the single-turn dataset $\mathcal{D}$ that are labeled with intent $I_t$: \begin{equation}P(X_{1:T}, I_{1:T}) = P(I_1) P(X_1|I_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t|I_{t-1}) P(X_t|I_t) \label{eq:hmm_qi}\end{equation} After sampling intents and utterances according to this model, we obtain a sequence of questions $q_{1:T}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: While HMMs can model sequences of intents and generate corresponding utterances, they have significant limitations in capturing the dependencies present in natural human conversations.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically, classic HMMs assume that observations (utterances) are conditionally independent given the current state (intent), and that the next state depends only on the current state.
elements.sent-2411-074: This independence assumption neglects the rich context and dependencies in multi-turn dialogues, such as anaphora resolution and ellipsis.. For example:.
elements.sent-2411-075: Person A: "Did you see Mary yesterday?" Person B: "Yes, she was at the park." (Anaphora resolution: "she" refers to Mary) Person A: "Did she say anything interesting?" Person B: "Not much, just talked about her new job." Person A: "And John?" Person B: "Didn't see him." (Ellipsis: "I" is omitted from full response "I didn't see him".).
elements.sent-2411-076: In contrast, questions generated by the HMM tend to be standalone and overly explicit, often repeating context unnecessarily and lacking coherence between turns.
elements.sent-2411-077: Questions from different turns may also semantically or logically contradict each other due to the independence assumptions, resulting in unnatural conversation flows.
elements.sent-2411-078: To overcome these limitations, we integrate LLMs into the HMM framework, allowing us to generate dialogues that are coherent and context-aware.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specifically, we leverage the LLM's in-context learning ability to generate each user utterance $q_t$ and the corresponding chatbot response $a_t$, conditioning on the conversation history up to turn $t-1$.
elements.sent-2411-080: For question generation at turn $t$, we construct an instruction prompt $\mathcal{S _q(I_t, X_t)$ that includes the current intent $I_t$ and a sample question $X_t$ associated with $I_t$ (to provide context and clarify the intent).}
elements.sent-2411-081: This prompt guides the LLM to generate a user question $q_t$ that is coherent with the conversation history $h_t = \{(q_1, a_1), \dots, (q_{t-1 , a_{t-1 )\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The complete input prompt for question generation is: \begin{equation}P_q = \{S_q(I_t, X_t), h_t\}\end{equation} Similarly, for answer generation, we provide a simple guidance $\mathcal{S _a$ along with the conversation history and the current question $q_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The complete input prompt for answer generation is: \begin{equation}P_a = \{S_a, h_t, q_t\}\end{equation} By generating both questions and answers in this way, the LLM can utilize all previous utterances and intents, effectively capturing dependencies and producing more natural dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-084: Formally, we generalize Equation~\ref{eq:hmm_qi to: \begin{equation}P(q_{1:T}, I_{1:T}) = \, P(I_1) P(X_1 | I_1) P(q_1 | I_1, X_1) \notag \times \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t | I_{t-1}) P(X_t | I_t) P(a_{t-1} | q_{1:t-1}) \notag \times P(q_t | I_t, X_t, q_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) \label{eq:hmm_qi_sub}\end{equation} This formulation captures the dependencies between the generated questions and answers and their history, overcoming the limitations of classic HMMs.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}P(q_{1:T}, I_{1:T}) = \, P(I_1) P(X_1 | I_1) P(q_1 | I_1, X_1) \notag \times \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t | I_{t-1}) P(X_t | I_t) P(a_{t-1} | q_{1:t-1}) \notag \times P(q_t | I_t, X_t, q_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) \label{eq:hmm_qi_sub}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: This generative process results in a conversation: \begin{equation}C = \{(q_1, I_1, a_1), \dots, (q_T, I_T, a_T)\}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: For each conversation, we generate an alternative response for the last turn using a smaller LLM, namely Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct~\cite{dubey2024llama .}
elements.sent-2411-087: This produces an alternative conversation $C_{\text{llama }$, where the last answer is replaced with $a_T^{\text{llama }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: To evaluate and compare the quality of the responses, we perform a point-wise evaluation using GPT-4, asking it to rate the quality of the last answer in each conversation on a scale from 1 to 10, given the chat history and the last question, we use an input prompt: \begin{equation}P_e = \{S_e, h_T, q_T, a_T\}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S _e$ is the instruction for evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-089: By comparing the scores of $C$ and $C_{\text{llama }$, we can determine which answer is better.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This ranking information is useful for downstream tasks, such as training the MTIC model with multi-task contrastive learning, as described in Section~\ref{sec:mtic .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Using the proposed pipeline, we present MINT-E, a large multilingual intent-aware multi-turn e-commerce dialogues corpus.
elements.sent-2411-092: The statistics of MINT-E, together with two existing dialogue corpus, namely Ubuntu Dialogue Corpus (UDC) and MSDialog, is shown in Table \ref{table:statistics .}
elements.sent-2411-093: The statistics of MINT-E are obtained by averaging across all markets.
elements.sent-2411-094: Only markets with language separable by white spaces, such as BR, ID, MY, PH, SG, and VN, are included.
elements.sent-2411-095: As ID is the main market with the most traffic online, we observe the top 15 intents based on the question-level ratio in ID generated dataset.
elements.sent-2411-096: The top user intent is to check delivery status, which makes up to 13.8\% of the questions.
elements.sent-2411-097: We also observed a long-tailed distribution with the top 10 intents taking up more than 50\% of the questions.
elements.sent-2411-098: The detail distribution is included in Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-099: Notably, while MSDialog contains only 12 unique intents and UDC lacks intent annotations altogether, MINT-E encompasses 381 distinct intents.
elements.sent-2411-100: This extensive range allows for more granular intent classification, reflecting the complexity and variety of real-world user interactions in e-commerce settings.
elements.sent-2411-101: Moreover, MINT-E is a multilingual corpus, covering eight different markets with diverse languages.
elements.sent-2411-102: In contrast, both UDC and MSDialog are predominantly in English.
elements.sent-2411-103: This multilingual aspect of MINT-E makes it particularly valuable for developing and evaluating MTIC models in diverse linguistic environments, addressing the challenges associated with intent classification across different languages and cultures.
elements.sent-2411-104: Although MINT-E has fewer sessions and questions compared to UDC and MSDialog, the data is richer in terms of intent diversity and domain specificity.
elements.sent-2411-105: The average number of words per question in MINT-E is around 11, similar to UDC, which suggests concise and natural conversational exchanges typical in chatbot interactions.
elements.sent-2411-106: MSDialog, on the other hand, has a much higher average of 75.91 words per question, indicating longer and more complex queries that may not reflect typical user behavior in e-commerce platforms.
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, MINT-E focuses specifically on e-commerce dialogues, providing domain-specific context that is highly relevant for training and evaluating chatbot systems in this sector.
elements.sent-2411-108: The corpus includes real-world scenarios and user intents that are common in online shopping experiences, making it a practical resource for both researchers and practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-109: In summary, MINT-E's advantages over benchmark datasets like UDC and MSDialog include:.
elements.sent-2411-110: Extensive Intent Coverage: With 381 unique intents, MINT-E allows for detailed and nuanced intent classification, capturing the complexities of user interactions in e-commerce.
elements.sent-2411-111: Multilingual Support: Covering eight markets and languages, MINT-E addresses the need for multilingual MTIC models, facilitating research in global contexts.
elements.sent-2411-112: Domain Specificity: Focused on e-commerce, the dataset provides relevant and practical dialogues that are directly applicable to industry needs.
elements.sent-2411-113: Realistic Conversational Data: The average length of questions and the number of turns per session reflect actual user behavior, enhancing the applicability of models trained on this data.
elements.sent-2411-114: As a rich, diverse, and practical dataset, MINT-E stands as a valuable resource that overcomes the limitations of existing corpora, supporting the development of more accurate and context-aware chatbot systems in multilingual and domain-specific applications.
elements.figure-2411-003: .95{ \begin{tabular}{|l| c c c|} Items UDC MSDialog MINT-E [0.5ex] \# Intents - 12 381 \# Sessions 930k 35k 20k \# Questions 7.1M 300k 44k \# Words 100M 24M 482k \# Avg. \# questions per session 7.71 8.94 2.92 \# Avg. \# words per question 10.34 75.91 11.00 [1ex] \end{tabular}} \caption{Statistics of UDC, MSDialog, and MINT-E.} \label{table:statistics}
elements.sent-2411-115: To assess the effectiveness of the MINT-E corpus, we focus on a relevant downstream task: Multi-Turn Intent Classification (MTIC).
elements.sent-2411-116: This section details the MTIC task, introduces our proposed training framework MINT-CL, which incorporates multi-task contrastive learning, and describes the experimental settings used to evaluate our approach.
elements.sent-2411-117: In MTIC, the goal is to predict the user's intent at the current turn of a conversation by considering both the current utterance and the preceding dialogue context.
elements.sent-2411-118: Given a conversation with a sequence of user utterances $\{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t\}$, the task is to determine the intent $I_t$ associated with the latest utterance $q_t$, utilizing the information from all previous turns.
elements.sent-2411-119: The input of the model concatenates all user utterances into a single sequence $q_{\text{all }$, separated by commas: \begin{equation}q_{all} = ``<q_1>, <q_2>, \dots, <q_t>"\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The language model used for classification is designed to handle the hierarchical nature of intents, organized into a three-level taxonomy.
elements.sent-2411-121: To capture both local and global hierarchical information, we combine a label-attention mechanism with a hierarchical text classification (HTC) approach inspired by HiTIN~\cite{Zhu2023HiTINHT .}
elements.sent-2411-122: We utilize the XLM-RoBERTa-base model to obtain contextual embeddings of the input text.
elements.sent-2411-123: Specifically, we extract the embedding corresponding to the [CLS] token to represent the entire input sequence: \begin{equation}H = \Phi_{XLM-R}(q_{all})^{[CLS]} \in R^{d}\end{equation} where $\Phi_{\text{XLM-R }$ denotes the XLM-RoBERTa encoder, and $d$ is the dimension of the hidden state.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The encoder is further pre-trained on our in-domain corpus to enhance its representation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-125: To leverage local information at each level of the intent hierarchy, we employ a label-attention mechanism with separate classifier heads for each layer.
elements.sent-2411-126: For each layer $l$, we compute an intermediate representation $L_l$: \begin{equation}L_l =  H W^1_l + b^1_l, if  l = 1, (H \oplus L_{l-1}) W^1_l + b^1_l, if  l > 1, \end{equation} where $W^1_l \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d}$ for $l = 1$ and $W^1_l \in \mathbb{R ^{2d \times d}$ for $l > 1$, $b^1_l \in \mathbb{R ^{d}$, and $\oplus$ denotes concatenation.}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}L_l =  H W^1_l + b^1_l, if  l = 1, (H \oplus L_{l-1}) W^1_l + b^1_l, if  l > 1, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-127: This setup allows each layer to incorporate information from the previous layer's output.
elements.sent-2411-128: The logits for each layer are then calculated as: \begin{equation}H_{local}^l = L_l W^2_l + b^2_l\end{equation} where $W^2_l \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times |\mathcal{I _l|}$ and $b^2_l \in \mathbb{R ^{|\mathcal{I _l|}$, with $|\mathcal{I _l|$ being the number of classes at layer $l$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: To capture global hierarchical information, we integrate an HTC component inspired by HiTIN.
elements.sent-2411-130: In this approach, a tree network is constructed based on the intent taxonomy, and messages are propagated in a bottom-up manner.
elements.sent-2411-131: The text representation $H$ is broadcast to the leaf nodes, and through hierarchical message passing, we obtain global logits $H_{\text{global ^l$ for each layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: We combine the local and global logits for each layer: \begin{equation}Y_l = softmax\left( H_{local}^l + H_{global}^l \right)\end{equation} To ensure consistency across the hierarchy, we perform a beam search over the three layers to predict the final intent.
elements.sent-2411-133: To enhance the model's performance, we introduce MINT-CL, a multi-task learning framework that incorporates a response ranking task using contrastive learning for MTIC.
elements.sent-2411-134: This framework effectively aids LLM in learning more robust representations by distinguishing between high-quality and lower-quality responses.
elements.sent-2411-135: For each conversation, we generate two responses for the last turn: a high-quality response $a^+$ and a lower-quality response $a^-$.
elements.sent-2411-136: These responses are obtained from different models or configurations, such as using different language models or varying the generation settings.
elements.sent-2411-137: We create input pairs by concatenating the conversation history with each response, separated by the [SEP] token: \begin{equation}c^+ = q_{all} \; [SEP] \; a^+ \quad c^- = q_{all} \; [SEP] \; a^-\end{equation} These inputs are then encoded using the same encoder: \begin{equation}H_2^+ = \Phi_{XLM-R}(c^+)^{[CLS]} \quad H_2^- = \Phi_{XLM-R}(c^-)^{[CLS]}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-138: We employ a contrastive loss similar to \cite{Gao2020DialogueRR to encourage the model to assign higher scores to better responses: \begin{equation}L_{contrastive} = -\log \frac{\exp(L_2^+)}{\exp(L_2^+) + \exp(L_2^-)}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}L_2^+ = H_2^+ W_2 + b_2 \quad L_2^- = H_2^- W_2 + b_2\end{equation} and $W_2 \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times 1}$ and $b_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ are the weights and bias for the ranking task.}}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}L_2^+ = H_2^+ W_2 + b_2 \quad L_2^- = H_2^- W_2 + b_2\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-139: The final loss function is a weighted sum of the intent classification loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{intent }$ and the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{contrastive }$: \begin{equation}L = L_{intent} + \lambda L_{contrastive}\end{equation} Here, $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter that balances the contribution of the contrastive loss.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: Through manual tuning, we found that setting $\lambda = 0.3$ yields the best results.
elements.sent-2411-141: To evaluate our proposed methods, we conducted a series of experiments across datasets from eight different markets.
elements.sent-2411-142: The assessment employed two main metrics: the quality of the generated dialogues and the accuracy on the downstream MTIC tasks.
elements.sent-2411-143: This section outlines the datasets used, the experimental setups, the experimental results and discussion.
elements.figure-2411-004: 1{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} Market Language \# Intents \# Samples BR Portuguese (pt) 312 67k ID Indonesian (id) 503 162k MY English/Malay (en, ms) 473 72k PH English/Filipino (en, fil) 252 27k SG English (en) 347 63k TH Thai (th) 377 49k TW Traditional Chinese (zh-tw) 387 32k VN Vietnamese (vi) 399 188k \end{tabular}} \caption{Dataset statistics for each market, including primary languages, number of intents, and dataset sizes.} \label{table:data_train_test}
elements.sent-2411-144: We utilized e-commerce question-intent datasets from eight markets: Brazil (BR), Indonesia (ID), Malaysia (MY), the Philippines (PH), Singapore (SG), Thailand (TH), Taiwan (TW), and Vietnam (VN).
elements.sent-2411-145: Table~\ref{table:data_train_test provides details on the primary languages, number of intents, and the size of the datasets for each market.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Among these, SG, MY, and PH primarily use English, while the others communicate in their native languages during conversations.
elements.sent-2411-147: To extract domain-specific knowledge, we collected about 100,000 online chatbot sessions from each market.
elements.sent-2411-148: This data helped us estimate the market-specific turn distribution $P(T)$, initial intent distribution $P(I_1)$, and intent transition distribution $P(I_t | I_{t-1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-149: We evaluated the quality of the synthetic dialogues using the GPT-4 model.
elements.sent-2411-150: The assessment focused on language fluency, logical flow of topics, and the coherence of questions within each session.
elements.sent-2411-151: Conversations were rated on a scale from 1 to 10, where 1 indicated poor quality and 10 represented excellent quality.
elements.sent-2411-152: The overall score was calculated by averaging the ratings across all evaluated conversations.
elements.sent-2411-153: The prompt for this evaluation can be found in Appendix \ref{prompt:session_rating .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Since our task setting is relatively new, we compared our method against the following baselines:.
elements.sent-2411-155: Random: Intents and questions were randomly selected without any domain knowledge, although the turn distribution $P(T)$ was maintained.
elements.sent-2411-156: HMM: We used the classic HMM with random sampling for emissions, as outlined in Section~\ref{ori_hmm .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Golden: This baseline consisted of actual customer conversations sampled from online chat logs, serving as a reference for natural dialogue quality.
elements.sent-2411-158: To further validate our approach, we test the models on the MTIC task.
elements.sent-2411-159: In this task, given a conversation history of user questions $h_t$ and the last question $q_t$, the goal is to predict the intent $I_t$ of the last question using all previous questions $q_{1...t-1}$ as context.
elements.sent-2411-160: The test sets were drawn from online chat logs, with the intents of the last questions manually annotated.
elements.sent-2411-161: Earlier questions in the conversations were not annotated.
elements.sent-2411-162: We excluded single-turn conversations to focus on the multi-turn aspect.
elements.sent-2411-163: We designed several experiments to understand the impact of our methods.
elements.sent-2411-164: As a starting point, we trained the model using only the single-turn question-intent pairs from our datasets.
elements.sent-2411-165: The model was optimized using standard cross-entropy loss for intent classification.
elements.sent-2411-166: To evaluate the effect of multi-turn data, we added the generated multi-turn dialogues from the MINT-E corpus to the training set.
elements.sent-2411-167: Each multi-turn conversation was broken down into training samples by concatenating the conversation history up to each turn: \begin{equation}\{ (q_1, I_1), (q_1, q_2, I_2), \dots, (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t, I_t) \}\end{equation} This approach allowed the model to learn from the context provided by earlier turns.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\{ (q_1, I_1), (q_1, q_2, I_2), \dots, (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t, I_t) \}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-168: We explored two multi-task learning strategies to enhance the model's performance:.
elements.sent-2411-169: Binary Classification for Response Ranking (RR): We introduced an auxiliary task where the model predicts whether a given response is of high quality.
elements.sent-2411-170: Responses were labeled as high-quality ($1$) or lower-quality ($0$), and the model was trained using binary cross-entropy loss alongside the intent classification loss.
elements.sent-2411-171: Contrastive Learning for Response Ranking (CR): Instead of assigning labels, we used contrastive learning to teach the model to differentiate between better and worse responses.
elements.sent-2411-172: The model learned to assign higher scores to superior responses within a pair.
elements.sent-2411-173: For both multi-task learning approaches, the intent classification and response ranking tasks shared the same encoder weights (XLM-RoBERTa-base).
elements.sent-2411-174: To balance the number of samples for the response ranking task, we randomly selected responses from different intents to serve as negative examples.
elements.sent-2411-175: We fine-tuned the XLM-RoBERTa-base model with a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-5}$, using a batch size of 32 over three epochs.
elements.sent-2411-176: The hyperparameter $\lambda$, which controls the weight of the contrastive loss, was set to 0.3 based on validation results.
elements.sent-2411-177: Table~\ref{table:results_rating shows the quality ratings for the generated dialogues across different markets.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Our generated dataset, MINT-E, consistently outperformed the baselines in every market.
elements.sent-2411-179: Interestingly, our generated dialogues sometimes received higher average ratings than the golden set.
elements.sent-2411-180: As users often provide brief or out-of-context queries, whereas the language model generates more detailed and contextually relevant questions.
elements.sent-2411-181: To illustrate, here are examples transitioning from the intent "Cancel order" to "Expedite delivery": Example 1 (Human) - Rating: 4.
elements.sent-2411-183: The dialogue in the human example lacks coherence, while the LLM-generated example provides context and a logical flow between the two intents.

elements.sent-2411-184: The impact of different training configurations on the MTIC task is presented in Table~\ref{table:results_downstream .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Incorporating multi-turn data generally improved performance compared to using only single-turn data.
elements.sent-2411-186: The multi-task learning strategies improved average accuracy by 0.5\% across 5 markets, particularly in English-speaking markets.
elements.sent-2411-187: However, in some non-English markets like Indonesia (ID) and Taiwan (TW), including multi-turn classification data didn't consistently boost performance.
elements.sent-2411-188: This might be due to limitations in the language model's ability to handle specific languages, which affect the quality of generated dialogues, further discussed in section 6.2.
elements.sent-2411-189: Instead, adding only the response ranking data trained with multi-task learning achieves better results on TW and ID.
elements.sent-2411-190: Because we generate 2 responses via 2 different LLM for response evaluation separately, increasing the chance of generating a proper answer.
elements.sent-2411-191: The response ranking as an auxiliary task can also not affect the classification task directly.
elements.sent-2411-192: With appropriate $\lambda$ setting for loss combination, we can avoid Seesaw Effect \cite{Ruder2017AnOO on multi-task learning and alleviate poor generation quality issues on non-English markets.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Our experiments show that integrating domain knowledge with LLM, as done in MINT-CL, noticeably enhances dialogue quality and improves performance on downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-194: The results suggest that leveraging multi-turn data and multi-task learning is beneficial, especially in markets where the language model performs well.
elements.sent-2411-195: However, challenges persist in low-resource languages where LLMs are less effective.
elements.sent-2411-196: Future work could focus on improving language models for these languages or exploring alternative methods to generate high-quality dialogues in multilingual settings.

elements.sent-2411-197: We examine the impact of individual components in the generation pipeline, specifically the HMM base model and the LLM enhancement, on overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-198: Firstly, we explore using HMM without LLM enhancement by randomly sampling utterances from similar questions under the intents generated by HMM.
elements.sent-2411-199: As shown in Table~\ref{table:results_rating , the dialogue quality decreases by an average of 0.88 points without LLM generation.}
elements.sent-2411-200: This drop occurs because the original HMM is limited by its emission independence, generating utterances conditioned solely on the current intent without considering prior context.
elements.sent-2411-201: Nevertheless, the HMM-only variant still achieves competitive performance compared to the golden baseline, demonstrating the strength of intent modeling.
elements.subsubsection-2411-022: Impact of Removing the Chain of Intent
elements.sent-2411-202: Next, we investigate the effect of removing the "chain of intent" generated by the HMM, which relies on prior distribution data from real chat logs.
elements.sent-2411-203: When the Markovian constraint is removed and intent is randomly sampled at each turn, we observe a decline in the natural flow of conversation.
elements.sent-2411-204: This results in reduced human-likeness, limited topic diversity, and a lack of authentic multi-turn conversational dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-205: Despite this, high-quality intent sequences remain effective in generating intent-aware dialogues, supporting prior findings that adding intent structure improves dialogue generation quality~\cite{kong2024platolm, Soudani2024ASO .}
elements.sent-2411-206: Table~\ref{table: multilingual result illustrates that MINT-CL shows weaker results on classification tasks in low-resource languages where LLM-generated content may be of lower quality.}
elements.sent-2411-207: We investigated this issue from two key aspects: token fertility and average words per sentence.
elements.sent-2411-208: Token fertility, as highlighted in prior studies~\cite{Rust2020HowGI , measures the number of sub-words generated per word when tokenizing.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Higher token fertility typically results in lower generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-210: For instance, \cite{Nguyen2023SeaLLMsL found that Thai (TH) and Vietnamese (VN) languages require 9X and 4X more tokens, respectively, compared to English when using ChatGPT's tokenizer.}
elements.sent-2411-211: This higher tokenization complexity negatively impacts generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-212: Similarly, Brazilian Portuguese (BR) is not encoded efficiently by LLMs without additional training data, as it differs significantly from European Portuguese in terms of vocabulary, grammar, pronunciation, and cultural expressions~\cite{kluge2024teenytinyllama .}
elements.sent-2411-213: These linguistic challenges lead to degraded generation quality and performance drops in low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-214: Additionally, we observe that the average number of words per question in MINT-E exceeds that of the golden dataset by 4 words, as shown in Table~\ref{table:statistics_golden .}
elements.sent-2411-215: This discrepancy in sentence length may introduce extraneous information during model training, further affecting classification task performance.
elements.sent-2411-216: Overall, these analyses confirm that LLM generation quality plays a crucial role in the effectiveness of our pipeline, particularly in low-resource language scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-217: Multi-turn Dialogue Generation (MTDG) emerge from both academics and industry due to its relevance to real-world applications such as chatbots and information-seeking systems~\cite{liu2024responsible .}
elements.sent-2411-218: However, they face challenges such as the data sparsity \cite{Zhang2018TailoredST, Zhang2018ReinforcingCF, Li2017AdversarialLF .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Moreover, MTDG requires more complex information and constraints \cite{zhang2020modeling, Chen2018HierarchicalVM, Zhang2018ContextSensitiveGO , posing additional challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In general, dialogue generation are categorised in open-domain generation and task-oriented generation \cite{lv-etal-2023-dialogps .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Open-domain generation is a context-sensitive process that spans multiple turns, with the model learning to generate appropriate yet open-ended responses based on previous utterances (i.e., the context), which can be categorized into preLLM and postLLM \cite{soudani2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-222: PreLLM methods include the HRED \cite{Serban2015BuildingED series of models.}
elements.sent-2411-223: This hierarchical encoder-decoder framework effectively captures contextual information and incorporates latent variables as intermediate states to generate diverse responses, such as variable HRED \cite{Serban2016AHL , MrRNN \cite{Serban2016MultiresolutionRN and DialoGPS\cite{lv-etal-2023-dialogps .}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Other related research adopts deep-learning models \cite{santhanam2019survey enhanced by internal knowledge to ensure on-topic response generation\cite{yu2022survey, madotto2018mem2seq, wang2020improving .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Popular architecture includes recurrent neural network(RNN) \cite{li2015diversity , memory network \cite{chen2018hierarchical and transformer \cite{lin2020hierarchical, olabiyi2023multi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Those works introduce topic words \cite{xing2017topic , emotion \cite{song2019generating, zhou2018emotional , and persona \cite{zhang2018personalizing, zhou2018emotional into the generation process to help deliver better dialogue responses.}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Most of the research still adopts supervised models, which require much training data for better performance.
elements.sent-2411-228: The advent of LLM has created new possibilities for automated text generation.
elements.sent-2411-229: PostLLM methods leverage powerful LLM to utilize prompt or instruction to generate instruction-response pairs automatically.
elements.sent-2411-230: Due to the excellent generation ability of LLM, most research on generation tasks focuses on developing high-quality instruction for multi-turn generation \cite{wang2022self, ding2023enhancing and enhancing the multi-turn instruction following ability with human feedback \cite{Sun2023ParrotEM, ouyang2022training .}}
elements.sent-2411-231: However, those open-domain generation methods didn't explicitly adopt the domain-specific intent into dialogue generation, so their work is hardly used in other downstream NLP tasks, such as intent or emotion classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-232: Task-oriented generation is goal-driven and limited to specific domains for task-oriented dialogue(TOD) \cite{soudani2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-233: It has been widely deployed in many industries as it delivers more efficient customer support \cite{moghe2022multi3nlu++, deng2022user .}
elements.sent-2411-234: The popular approach centres around user simulation \cite{lin2021domain, tseng2021transferable , where two agents interact to process the dialogue history and create a belief state for each side of the conversation, called self-play.}
elements.sent-2411-235: They adopt HMM \cite{Cuayhuitl2005HumancomputerDS , partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDP), as a user model for spoken dialogue generation.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Recent work also adopts LLM as a user simulator \cite{kong2024platolm with reinforcement learning \cite{min_et_al_2023 and in-context learning framework \cite{terragni2023context, askari2024self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: This work considers the limited number of dialogue acts, which can't include all types of intents within the chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-238: Multi-Turn Intent Classification focuses on understanding user intents within the context of ongoing dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-239: To enhance interpretability in intent identification, \citet{yang2020reinforcement utilized reinforcement learning over knowledge graphs, demonstrating improved performance in multi-turn settings.}
elements.sent-2411-240: To address the complexity of multi-turn conversational contexts in the e-commerce domain, \citet{junhua2024lara developed LARA, a Linguistic-Adaptive Retrieval-Augmentation framework across multiple languages.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Furthermore, \citet{fan2022building developed a system leveraging pre-trained language models for fine-grained query understanding, aligning with our focus on intent-aware dialogues in specific domains.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Additionally, \citet{hao2023intentdial introduced a method employing reinforcement learning to construct an intent graph for identification, highlighting the importance of capturing intent transitions in dialogues.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Effective Multi-Turn Intent Classification (MTIC) in chatbot systems requires large-scale, domain-specific, multilingual multi-turn dialogue datasets.
elements.sent-2411-244: Creating such datasets require tremendous time and resources.
elements.sent-2411-245: To tackle this challenge, we first proposed Chain-of-Intent mechanism, which leverages LLM-enhanced HMMs to model sequences of user intents based on prior knowledge extracted from historical chat logs.
elements.sent-2411-246: By sampling intent transitions and turn distributions, the LLM effectively capture realistic intent flows and generates coherent and contextually appropriate multi-turn dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-247: Furthermore, we introduced MINT-CL, a framework that employs multi-task contrastive learning for MTIC, which is able to learn robust representations by incorporating additional training signals, thereby improving intent classification accuracy in multilingual and resource-constrained settings.
elements.sent-2411-248: Lastly, we released MINT-E, a multilingual, intent-aware, multi-turn e-commerce dialogue corpus generated using our framework.
elements.sent-2411-249: Our experiments demonstrate promising results in quality evaluation of the dataset and model performance for MTIC downstream tasks across languages.
elements.sent-2411-250: The prompts for GPT-4 will typically involve three distinct roles: system, user, and assistant.
elements.sent-2411-251: In the following example, the messages for each role are clearly distinguished by their respective role, denoted by [{role] tags.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Variables are encapsulated in between "\{" and "\}" symbols.
elements.sent-2411-253: Your task is to complete the conversation with an enquiry with intention {cn .}
elements.sent-2411-254: This is a conversation with a chatbot, so make it informal.
elements.sent-2411-255: Besides, your enquiry should be related and continuous of the previous context only if possible, that is, without deviating from the definition of example enquiries.
elements.sent-2411-256: However, it is important to refer to the example enquiries below, strictly follow their meaning and scope when asking enquiries.
elements.sent-2411-257: The meaning of the inquiry should be consistent with the intention after considering the context.
elements.sent-2411-258: Answer in the language used in your previous enquiry.
elements.sent-2411-259: You may use coreference to make it more natural, substitute the subject mentioned in previous enquiries with pronouns.
elements.sent-2411-260: For example, you may say "Cancel it" instead of "Cancel the ShopeeFood order" if "ShopeeFood" had been mentioned by you before.
elements.sent-2411-261: Most importantly, DO NOT deviate from the meaning of the example enquiries, follow the definition faithfully, do not add unrelated information.
elements.sent-2411-262: Lastly, for your attention, the utterance should have intention {intent .}
elements.sent-2411-263: Omit the details included in the previous enquiries and DO NOT start with acknowledgement like "ok", "i see", "one more question", "sorry" etc, keep it simple.
elements.sent-2411-264: Example enquiries for the intent: - {sampled single-turn question 1 - {sampled single-turn question 2 - {sampled single-turn question 3 Remember, you are the customer, DO NOT act like you are providing help.}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: DO NOT thank the chatbot.. DO NOT apologize.
elements.sent-2411-266: Rules: - You always have the solution, be helpful to the user's enquiries.
elements.sent-2411-267: Your evaluation should consider factors such as the helpfulness, relevance, accuracy, depth, creativity, and level of detail of the response.
elements.sent-2411-269: Rate the response on a scale of 1 to 10.
elements.sent-2411-270: DO NOT provide any explanation, strictly return only a rating.
elements.sent-2411-271: The conversation generated happens on an e-commerce platform, it includes enquiries from a customer directed to a customer service chatbot (cs chatbot).Your evaluation should consider factors such as fluency of language, topic flow reasonableness, and continuity of messages.
elements.sent-2411-272: Furthermore, The language used shouldn't matter in this test.
elements.sent-2411-273: Rate the quality on a scale of 1 to 10, you may use this as the standard: 1 > Extremely not fluent and unnatural.
elements.sent-2411-274: There is abrupt change of topic between enquiries with no logical connection to previous dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-275: There is any contradiction in the enquiries.
elements.sent-2411-276: In conversational AI systems, a critical challenge in training effective multi-turn intent classification models lies in the generation of large-scale, domain-specific, multilingual dialogue datasets.
elements.sent-2411-277: In this paper, we introduce Chain-of-Intent, a novel framework that integrates Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) with Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate intent-driven, context-aware dialogues through self-play.
elements.sent-2411-278: Our method first extracts domain-specific intent transition patterns from real-world e-commerce chat logs, which guide the modeling of turn-level dynamics and intent sequences.
elements.sent-2411-279: LLMs are then employed to parameterize the emission probabilities of HMMs, enabling the generation of natural, coherent utterances aligned with predicted intents and dialogue context.
elements.sent-2411-280: We further propose MINT-CL, a multi-task contrastive learning framework for multi-turn intent classification, which improves performance while reducing dependence on large-scale annotated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-281: Empirical results demonstrate that our approach outperforms competitive baselines in both dialogue generation quality and classification accuracy, particularly in multilingual settings.
elements.sent-2411-282: To facilitate future research, we release MINT-E, a comprehensive, multilingual, intent-aware multi-turn dialogue corpus derived from the e-commerce domain\footnote{The reproduced source code and dataset are available at \url{https://github.com/junhua/chain-of-intent .. .}}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=.45\textwidth]{figures/challenges.png} \caption{Example of intent-aware multi-turn dialogue and the challenges of annotating single-turn cross-market intents to create a multi-turn intent classification dataset. The intents (e.g., Chitchat - Complaint) are in the format of <intent type>-<intent name>.} \label{fig:challenge}
elements.sent-2411-283: Users typically interact with these chatbots through multi-turn conversations to achieve their goals.
elements.sent-2411-284: Like many deep learning approaches~\cite{Soudani2024ASO , MTIC models heavily rely on large amounts of domain-specific multi-turn intent-utterance pairs to boost their performance~\cite{ni2023recent .}}
elements.sent-2411-285: However, collecting such datasets efficiently, especially across different languages, is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-286: Figure~\ref{fig:challenge illustrates the challenges in annotating multi-turn dialogues.}
elements.sent-2411-287: This vast number of intents significantly increases the complexity of both classification tasks and data annotation.
elements.sent-2411-288: However, there are FAQ, chit-chat, and TOD intents in multi-turn sessions shown in Figure ~\ref{fig:challenge , so considering only TOD intents is limited for dialogue generation in real-world scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-289: To address these issues, this paper contributes three solutions from different aspects, as follows: Contribution 1: Chain-of-Intent.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=.48\textwidth]{figures/Methods.png} \caption{Overview of the Chain-of-Intent framework: using LLM-enhanced HMM to sample intent sequences and generate intent-aware multi-turn dialogues.} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-290: Figure~\ref{fig:method shows the detailed workflow of intent-aware dialogue generation, which comprises two main stages: domain knowledge extraction and question generation.}
elements.sent-2411-291: There are two types of domain-specific knowledge to account for when generating a conversation, namely the number of turns in each conversation and the intent transition from intents of historical questions.
elements.sent-2411-292: Just like the number of turns, the intent transition information is also gathered from the chat logs using Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 0.1$ to handle unseen transitions.
elements.sent-2411-293: That said, by the Law of Large Numbers, if we gather the statistics from a reasonably large number of chat logs, the intent transition matrix obtained will provide a good estimation of true intents.
elements.sent-2411-294: Using these distributions, we first sample the number of turns $T$ from $P(T)$, then sample the initial intent $I_1$ from $P_{\text{init }$, and subsequent intents $I_t$ from $P_{\text{trans (\cdot|I_{t-1 )$ conditioned on the previous intent.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-295: Formally, we define the HMM as: \begin{equation}P(I_{1:T}, q_{1:T}) = P(I_1) P(q_1|I_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t|I_{t-1}) P(q_t|I_t)\end{equation} where $I_t$ corresponds to the intent at turn $t$, and $q_t$ corresponds to the user's utterance (question) at turn $t$, with normalization constraints $\sum P(T) = 1$ and $\sum P(I_t|I_{t-1 ) = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-296: The observation model $P(q_t|I_t)$ is modeled by uniformly sampling a question $X_t$ from the set of questions in the single-turn dataset $\mathcal{D}$ that are labeled with intent $I_t$: \begin{equation}P(X_{1:T}, I_{1:T}) = P(I_1) P(X_1|I_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t|I_{t-1}) P(X_t|I_t) \label{eq:hmm_qi}\end{equation} After sampling intents and utterances according to this model, we obtain a sequence of questions $q_{1:T}$.
elements.sent-2411-297: To incorporate contextual dependencies, we extend the formulation in Equation~\ref{eq:hmm_qi by conditioning question generation on conversation history: \begin{equation}P(q_{1:T}, I_{1:T}) = \, P(I_1) P(X_1 | I_1) P(q_1 | I_1, X_1) \notag \times \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(I_t | I_{t-1}) P(X_t | I_t) P(a_{t-1} | q_{1:t-1}) \notag \times P(q_t | I_t, X_t, q_{1:t-1}, a_{1:t-1}) \label{eq:hmm_qi_sub}\end{equation} This formulation captures the dependencies between the generated questions and answers and their history, overcoming the limitations of classic HMMs.}
elements.figure-2411-011: .95{ \begin{tabular}{|l| c c c|} Items UDC MSDialog MINT-E [0.5ex] \# Intents - 12 381 \# Sessions 930k 35k 20k \# Questions 7.1M 300k 44k \# Words 100M 24M 482k \# Avg. \# questions per session 7.71 8.94 2.92 \# Avg. \# words per question 10.34 75.91 11.00 [1ex] \end{tabular}} \caption{Comparison of dataset statistics for UDC, MSDialog, and MINT-E.} \label{table:statistics}
elements.sent-2411-298: Based on validation performance across three values $\{0.1, 0.3, 0.5\}$, we set $\lambda = 0.3$.
elements.sent-2411-299: While this approach may exhibit preference bias toward GPT-generated content, it provides consistent comparative assessment across methods.
elements.sent-2411-300: Interestingly, our generated dialogues sometimes received higher average ratings than the golden set, as users often provide brief or out-of-context queries, whereas the language model generates more detailed and contextually relevant questions.

elements.sent-2411-301: The multi-task learning strategies improved average accuracy by 0.5\% on average across 5 markets, particularly in English-speaking markets.

elements.sent-2411-303: Multi-turn Dialogue Generation (MTDG) emerges from both academia and industry due to its relevance to real-world applications, such as chatbots and information-seeking systems, in production~\cite{liu2024balancing,liu2024responsible .}
elements.sent-2411-304: Moreover, MTDG requires more complex information and constraints \cite{zhang2020modeling, Chen2018HierarchicalVM, Zhang2018ContextSensitiveGO,mu2023modelling , posing additional challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-305: In general, dialogue generation is categorised into open-domain generation and task-oriented generation \cite{lv-etal-2023-dialogps .}
elements.sent-2411-306: Other related research adopts deep-learning models \cite{santhanam2019survey enhanced by internal knowledge to ensure on-topic response generation\cite{yu2022survey, madotto2018mem2seq, wang2020improving,mu2024label,mu2022revision .}}
elements.sent-2411-307: Popular architectures include recurrent neural networks (RNNs) \cite{li2015diversity , memory networks \cite{Chen2018HierarchicalVM and transformers \cite{lin2020hierarchical,olabiyi2023multi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Due to the excellent generation ability of LLM, most research on generation tasks focuses on developing high-quality instruction for multi-turn generation \cite{wang2022self,ding2023enhancing and enhancing the multi-turn instruction following ability with human feedback \cite{Sun2023ParrotEM,ouyang2022training .}}
elements.sent-2411-309: However, these open-domain generation methods do not explicitly adopt domain-specific intents into dialogue generation, so their work is hardly used in other downstream NLP tasks, such as intent or emotion classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-310: Task-oriented generation is goal-driven and limited to specific domains for task-oriented dialogue (TOD) \cite{soudani2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-311: It has been widely deployed in many industries as it delivers more efficient customer support \cite{moghe2022multi3nlu++,deng2022user .}
elements.sent-2411-312: Recent work also adopts LLM as a user simulator \cite{kong2024platolm with reinforcement learning \cite{min_et_al_2023 and in-context learning framework \cite{terragni2023context,Askari2024SelfseedingAM .}}}

elements.sent-2411-316: We employ generative AI as part of the data augmentation process within our proposed framework, as detailed in earlier sections of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-317: We also occasionally used generative AI tools to assist with grammar, spelling and minor edits on text originally written by the authors.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-318: We also propose MINT-CL, a multi-task contrastive learning framework for multi-turn intent classification, which improves performance while reducing dependence on large-scale annotated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-319: Empirical results demonstrate that our approach outperforms competitive baselines in dialogue generation quality and classification accuracy, particularly in multilingual settings.
